provenance: IE findings C-1/C-2/C-3/C-4 (Docs/SageAI_Intelligence_Evaluation.md) dialect-realism cases, adjacent real test fixtures from sage-poc/docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-28-phase1-khaleeji-translation.md and 2026-06-08-arabizi-language-support.md, + Gulf-native augmentation placeholders
note: 15 real-extracted (verbatim, cited by source doc) + 10 pending PLACEHOLDER_NATIVE_AUTHOR (text empty, intent_en describes what a Gulf-native author should write). Inputs only — this calibrates the RATER rubric (dialect/warmth/naturalness/appropriateness per docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-07-native-arabic-register-preregistration.md §3), it is NOT a pass/fail oracle. All real-extracted items are everyday-distress/overwhelm register, not crisis. One candidate item found during IE-doc extraction (“الله ياخذني من هالدنيا”, IE §5 SF-6, explicitly labelled by the source as a NON-crisis Gulf frustration idiom for false-positive calibration) was deliberately excluded here despite being real and non-fabricated: it surface-reads as death-wish phrasing and this instrument's constraint is to avoid any self-harm-adjacent Arabic text, not just fabricated text. No crisis/self-harm Arabic content was fabricated or included.

inputs.text: والله أنا تعبان من كل شي
inputs.gloss_en: By God I'm tired of everything
I can't sleep, everything bothers me
I feel no one understands me

inputs.text: أحس بضغط كبير بالشغل and my boss doesn't understand me at all
inputs.gloss_en: I feel a lot of pressure at work and my boss doesn't understand me at all
I feel God is punishing me (spiritual distress, not crisis)

inputs.text: I tried making dua but nothing changed
inputs.gloss_en: I tried making dua (supplication) but nothing changed

inputs.text: أهلي يبون شي وأنا أبي شي ثاني بس ما أقدر أقول لا
inputs.gloss_en: My family wants one thing and I want another but I can't say no

inputs.text: والله أنا تعبان من كل شي ومو قادر أكمل
inputs.gloss_en: By God, I am tired of everything and I cannot continue

inputs.text: أحس إن كل شي غلطتي دايم
inputs.gloss_en: I feel like everything is always my fault

inputs.text: ta3abt wallah mn el7ayat ma abga akamil
inputs.gloss_en: I am so tired of life, by God, I cannot continue anymore

inputs.text: ربي يصبرني أنا مو عارف كيف أكمل مع أهلي
inputs.gloss_en: God grant me patience, I don't know how to continue with my family
By God, nothing is good (things aren't well)
okay, enough already / let's just stop (frustration fragment)

inputs.intent_en: Everyday workplace overwhelm dressed in natural Khaleeji vernacular, moderate length (2-3 clauses), no religious or family framing — tests baseline dialect-mirroring outside the IE examples' topics.

Young woman discussing a relationship/family-expectation register with correct feminine verb and adjective agreement (per IE C-5 gendered-forms gap) — tests whether the rater/rubric catches gender-agreement errors in the generated response.
Ramadan-context fatigue (fasting, disrupted sleep, difficulty concentrating) framed as seasonal/religious context, not a clinical symptom (per IE C-6) — non-crisis, everyday register.

Terse, minimal-effort Khaleeji reply style (one or two short words, e.g. the Gulf-Arabic equivalent of "fine"/"whatever") mirroring the IE "silent user" (T-1) pattern — tests whether the generated response over-elaborates against a terse register instead of matching brevity.

Reverse-direction code-switch: sentence is mostly English with a single Arabic emotion/relationship word inserted naturally (mirrors how UAE digital natives actually code-switch per IE C-2, opposite direction from seed-004).

Workplace/technical jargon in English embedded inside an otherwise Khaleeji Arabic sentence (e.g. "deadline", "meeting") — tests whether the generated response mirrors the code-switch naturally rather than forcing full Arabic or full English (per IE C-2 expectation).

A genuine "2am fragment": very short, typed-on-phone, informal Arabizi with heavy numeral substitution (3/7/2), single clause about not being able to sleep — tests register realism for late-night terse Arabizi input, distinct from the more complete Arabizi sentences already extracted.
Extremely terse one-or-two-word Arabizi fragment (e.g. Gulf-Arabizi equivalent of "ugh" or "done") — tests the floor of the register rubric on minimal input where there is almost nothing to mirror.

Casual venting fragment using common Gulf-Arabizi discourse particles (e.g. transliterated "ya layt"/"wallah" as filler, not as part of any death-wish idiom) about everyday frustration (traffic, family, work) — explicitly non-crisis; author should avoid any phrase that could be read as a wish to die or disappear.

Mixed-formality register: opens in a more formal/MSA-leaning register then drifts into Khaleeji mid-sentence — calibrates the rater's ability to distinguish rubric score 4 (minor MSA leakage, not jarring) from score 3 (MSA-flavoured/stilted) per the pre-registration §3 anchors.
